title: deferred playbook executor with scheduled dry-run and safe rollback hooks
preview: Motivation: On-call automation needs a way to schedule runbooks to execute outside peak windows, run a non-destructive dry-run before live execution, and ensure safe automatic rollback when automation makes state changes. This PR adds a Deferred Playbook Executor that accepts scheduled windows and optional dry-run payloads, a scheduled dry-run runner that simulates playbook steps without side effects, and pluggable safe-rollback hooks that capture pre-change snapshots and provide an automated revert path. Key design points: (1) All deferred executions are stored as immutable scheduling tasks with idempotency tokens, (2) dry-run mode routes actions to a simulator layer that returns predicted outcomes and a list of mutated resources, (3) rollback hooks register resource snapshotters and revert actions, and (4) RBAC/feature-flag gating prevents accidental live scheduling. This change also adds telemetry (metrics and logs) for scheduled_task.created, scheduled_task.executed, dry_run.result, and rollback.invoked, plus tracing tags for schedule_id and runbook_id. Rollout: feature-flag gated, initial rollout to staging for 2 weeks with synthetic scheduled dry-run tests, then canary to 10% of production namespaces. See linked Linear items for acceptance criteria and SLO checks.

title: Support attaching retention config export to audit log evidence package
preview:
Context
Customers (and GRC internally) frequently request both (a) audit logs for a specified window and (b) an export of the tenant’s retention configuration (incl. defaults + overrides + change history pointer) as a single evidence package. This PR adds a first-class way to bundle the retention config export alongside the audit log export output.

Scope
- Add optional attachment support to evidence packages produced by audit-log-exporter.
- Implement a RetentionConfigAttachmentProvider which fetches retention config export from the control plane export endpoint and embeds it in the package.
- Update report template runner to allow "attachments" definitions (initially: retention_config).
- Add guardrails: size limits, explicit allowlist, and redaction check for known sensitive fields.
- Update CLI + HTTP API to accept the attachment flag(s).

Design notes
- Attachments are stored within the package as additional files under evidence/attachments/.
- Retention config is included as JSON (versioned) + a small README describing what it is and how it’s generated.
- The exporter is intentionally not doing policy evaluation; it only fetches the export artifact produced by the retention policy service (control plane).
- All attachment requests are recorded in the exporter’s own audit event stream (EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_ADDED) for traceability.

Usage
HTTP API:
- POST /v1/exports
  - body: { "template_id": "customer_evidence_v1", "time_window": {"start": "...", "end": "..."}, "attachments": ["retention_config"] }

CLI:
- audit-log-exporter export --template customer_evidence_v1 --start 2025-02-01T00:00:00Z --end 2025-02-15T00:00:00Z --attach retention_config

Validation / guardrails
- attachments must be from a static allowlist.
- retention config attachment requires control-plane auth scope: retention:export (in addition to audit:export).
- max attachment size: 2 MiB (hard fail; error code EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_TOO_LARGE).
- package builder refuses to overwrite an existing file path (defense-in-depth).

Testing
- Unit: attachment path sanitation, allowlist validation, size checks.
- Integration: mocked control plane export response (v1 + v1.1) and package contents verification.
- E2E (staging): export generated for Northbridge trial tenant; verified package contains audit_logs.csv + attachments/retention_config.json + attachments/README-retention-config.txt.

Rollout
- Feature-gated behind EXPORT_ATTACHMENTS_ENABLED (default off).
- Enabled in staging immediately; production enablement after Security sign-off on package contents.

References
- Linear: ENG-18434 (audit logs retention enforcement), ENG-18441 (compliance report pack generator)
- Support signal: SUP-3842 (enterprise customer requests retention controls + report pack)
- Related exporter work: PR-612 (time-windowed exports and filters)

Open questions (tracked in ENG-18441)
- Whether to include retention config change history in the attachment vs. only a pointer/link to download separately.
- Whether to also attach data residency/region summary as a separate attachment type (likely next).

title: introduce-mini-deterministic-benchmark-corpus-and-sparse-cpu-probe-reporter
preview:
Motivation: We lack a compact, deterministic benchmark fixture that reproduces common short-sequence hotpaths (latency-sensitive generations of length 8-64 tokens) while being cheap to run in CI and on local dev machines. Current suites are heavyweight and noisy; this PR introduces a curated mini corpus plus an experimental sparse CPU probe reporter that samples kernel-level wall-clock for a small set of annotated hotspots. The goal is faster benches that still exercise scheduling, KV-cache priming, and transient batch assembly so we can catch regressions early.

What this PR contains (high-level):
- mini_bench_corpus/: a compact set (12 sessions) of synthetic and real prompt traces sampled from production anonymized logs, normalized for determinism (fixed tokenization seeds, pinned random seeds, and controlled primer lengths).
- runner/mini_runner.py: lightweight harness that spins up the same runtime used by larger benches but with strict timing windows and a deterministic sampler to remove non-deterministic jitter.
- reporters/sparse_cpu_probe.py: an opt-in reporter that attaches a short-lived wall-clock probe to specific runtime phases (tokenize, model_dispatch, kv_priming, generate_step) and emits per-phase histograms only when the probe triggers (1% sampling by default). Designed to avoid adding noise to general benches.
- ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml: a small GitHub Actions workflow that runs the corpus on PRs with a ~6 minute budget; it compares p50/p95 against a baseline artifact and fails on regressions above threshold.

Design and rationale:
Determinism: each trace is annotated with a seed and a deterministic tokenization path; the runner enforces identical preconditions (no background threads, CPU governor hint) so runs are repeatable within CI environment variance bounds.
Sparse probing: instead of instrumenting every run, the reporter samples a tiny fraction and attaches a reproducible trace ID so if we see a regressed sample we can re-run the same trace at higher fidelity. This keeps overall noise low and avoids skewing aggregate numbers.
Compatibility: The code uses existing bench harness interfaces (Runner and Reporter base classes) so it's a non-invasive addition and can be toggled from larger suites.

Commits (summary):
- Add mini corpus with 12 session JSONs and deterministic token metadata.
- Implement mini_runner harness with deterministic scheduler and CLI flags (--seed, --reps, --noisy=false).
- Add sparse_cpu_probe reporter that uses perf_event_open fallback to a tight wall-clock sampler when perf unavailable.
- CI workflow to run the mini suite on PRs and store artifact diffs.
- Tests for determinism (unit test asserts repeatable token sequences across runs) and integration test for reporter output schema.

Files changed (top-level):
- benchmark-lab/mini_bench_corpus/ (12 files added)
- benchmark-lab/runner/mini_runner.py (+450 lines)
- benchmark-lab/reporters/sparse_cpu_probe.py (+180 lines)
- benchmark-lab/tests/test_mini_determinism.py (+120 lines)
- benchmark-lab/ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml (+60 lines)
- docs/bench.md (small addition linking the mini suite)
- README updates to document how to reproduce sampled failures locally

Testing and CI:
- Local unit tests added (pytest). Determinism test asserts identical token hashes across 3 runs with different wall-time but identical seeds.
- Integration regression check runs three repeated executions and computes p50/p95; CI workflow uploads an artifact 'mini-bench-baseline' on the first green run and compares subsequent runs to it.
- GitHub Actions matrix contains: ubuntu-latest (cpu-only), and an optional docker GPU job (manual).
- CI results: first run succeeded, a flaky timer test failed on second run due to CI jitter and was fixed by tighter sleep calibration; final CI status: pass.

Review thread (highlights):
- Mariana Lopez: "Nice small suite — can we reduce the default sampling from 1% to 0.5% to further lower overhead in cross-repo CI?"
- Riley Chen (author): "I tried 0.5% in an earlier iteration and saw loss of signal for 1-2 regressions; leaving at 1% for now but made sampling a CLI/config flag and added documentation on how to lower it per workflow."
- Ethan Park: "Can the reporter fall back gracefully when perf_event_open is not permitted in constrained runners? Also please ensure the reporter doesn't change scheduling affinity."
- Riley Chen: "Added fallback to monotonic wall-clock read with tighter sampling window; reporter only reads timestamps and never sets affinity. Added an integration test to validate behavior on restricted containers."
- Nora Singh: "Tests look good, but add a badge and baseline artifact upload so we can track long-term drift."
- Riley Chen: "Added artifact upload and a short section in docs/bench.md recommending baseline refresh cadence (monthly or after major compiler/toolchain upgrades)."

Performance & safety notes:
- This suite is explicitly not intended for capacity planning; it's a fast signal suite to detect regressions in hotpaths. For throughput/occupancy capacity planning, continue using the larger multi-stage suites.
- Reporter uses sampling with minimal overhead (<0.5% observed in local microbench runs).

How to reproduce a sampled failure locally:
1) Use runner/mini_runner.py --seed <trace-seed> --reps 10 --reporter sparse_cpu_probe --trace-id <id>
2) If the reporter indicated a regression sample, pass --high-fidelity to re-run that trace with full instrumentation and KV-cache warmup.

Follow-ups/TODOs:
- Add automated upload of sample traces to the artifact store for easier bisecting. (ENG-2178)
- Wire a dashboard in Redwood Console that surfaces mini-bench p50/p95 trends over time. (ENG-2104)
- Experiment with adaptive sampling rates tied to observed variance.

Merge notes:
- Squash-merged to main after final approval from Mariana and Ethan. Release notes added to the repo changelog. No configuration defaults were changed for existing suites.

title: Expedited rejoin stabilizer and controlled load-trim hotfix
preview: Motivation: Following a recent outage reconstruction (SUP-4512) we observed an oscillation during regional rejoin where multiple nodes aggressively tried to reclaim tenant KV slots, triggering cascade redistributions and transient SLO violations. This PR introduces a short-term hotfix to: 1) introduce a gentle rejoin backoff window (per-tenant jittered delay + exponential cap) to avoid synchronized reclaims; 2) add a controlled load-trim path that softly sheds low-priority traffic during rejoin windows; and 3) emit new telemetry to make oscillation and rejoin latency visible in the Console. This change is intentionally conservative: defaults apply only during detected rejoin epochs and are disabled outside of a rejoin recovery window. Changes: - runtime/scheduler/rejoin_coordinator.go: add rejoin state machine with randomized tenant backoff and exponential cooldown logic; add tests for staggered rejoin timings. - runtime/load_trim/trimmer.go: implement priority-aware soft-trimmer that signals request proxies to shed non-critical classes via header flag and emits approximate token savings. - controlplane/telemetry/v2: add rejoin_epoch metric, tenant_rejoin_backoff_seconds histogram, and soft_trim_shed_count counter. - config/defaults.yaml: new toggle rejoin_relay.enabled (default true for this rollout window) and rejoin_relay.default_backoff_ms. - docs/ops/rejoin-playbook.md: updated playbook with diagnostic queries and rollback steps. Rollout plan: - Stage: feature gated behind rejoin_relay.enabled. - Canary: enable for 5% of tenants in us-west-2 for 2 hours, observe rejoin_epoch and token/shed metrics. - Ramp: 25% after 6 hours if no increase in 95th latency or error rate; full rollout after 24h stability. Backout: flip rejoin_relay.enabled=false; will disable new path immediately. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for coordinator state transitions - [x] e2e simulation harness run for tenant rejoin storm - [x] metrics wired to Console dashboards - [x] docs updated - [x] post-merge telemetry alert to watch for increased shed volume Monitoring and alerts: new SLO probe rule added to detect >3x baseline soft_trim_shed_count in 10m window; alert routed to SRE runbook. Notes: This is a hotfix to reduce immediate customer impact while we finalize a larger, model-aware eviction redesign. See ENG-9821 for the long-running redesign epic.

title: responses-adapter: propagate determinism/seed headers and structured-output annotations
preview: Motivation: The OpenAI Responses variants that customers adopt increasingly rely on explicit determinism/seed directives and lightweight structured-output annotations passed through the HTTP layer. Without preserving those hints across our OpenAI-compat adapter we observed divergences between direct upstream behavior and Redwood-hosted responses in customer compatibility testing (see ENG-4821). This PR implements two coordinated changes in the adapter and runtime surface to improve compatibility and reduce customer surprises: 1) deterministic/seed propagation: accept and translate OpenAI-style determinism flags (seed, deterministic, x-seed) present as either JSON fields in the Responses body or as HTTP headers, and persist them through the routing, reassembly, and streaming layers so that streaming reassembly uses the same determinism hint used by the model selection logic; 2) structured-output annotations: accept structured output schema hints (openai_schema, schema_hint, or x-output-schema) and surface them to the structured-output postprocessor and the tool-call router. Implementation summary: - HTTP adapter: new header handling for X-OpenAI-Seed, X-OpenAI-Deterministic and X-OpenAI-Output-Schema; these are normalized to internal headers X-Redwood-Seed and X-Redwood-Output-Schema during internal RPCs so that downstream components see a consistent key. - Payload parsing: Responses-compatible body parsing now reads top-level "determinism" and "seed" keys in addition to existing generation params and merges them with header values (headers take precedence). - Streaming reassembly: streaming reassembly now attaches a deterministic-context token to each stream chunk and includes seed metadata in the final reassembly envelope so consumers and logs can assert determinism and replay. This prevents a class of flaky stream reorder bugs seen during canary. - Tool/function calling: the toolcall envelope now carries the propagated schema hint on the function metadata so third-party tool runners can perform schema-aware validation/dispatch. - Backwards compatibility: adapter will ignore unknown header keys and continues to accept legacy openai-style body shapes; a compatibility shim maps older "seed_hint" fields to the new internal header where detected. - Tests and validations: unit tests for adapter parsing, integration tests exercising streaming reassembly with seeded responses, and an end-to-end canary against a mocked OpenAI-responses backend were added. Checklist: - [x] Add header normalization in adapter layer - [x] Merge seed into routing decisions (with tests) - [x] Attach seed/annotations to streaming reassembly envelope - [x] Surface schema hint to toolcall metadata - [x] Add integration tests and canary job - [x] Update docs/compat notes (short note in docs site) Related: ENG-4821 (compat issues during customer test runs), ENG-4835 (structured output rollout plan). Breaking change: none; feature is opt-in via header/body keys. Rollout plan: feature gated behind a config flag responses.adapter.enable_seed_passthrough (default off). We enabled the flag in the dedicated canary namespaces and verified downstream regression metrics before merging.

title: holo-surge-sequencer-and-crossframe-occupancy-exporter
preview:
Motivation: we observed sustained mid-tail p95 instability when replaying multi-sequence conversational traffic with interleaved priming and varied KV cache hits. Existing synthetic runners produce either Poisson-like bursts or deterministic ramps; they do not emulate the fractal-like, multi-scale surges we see in production that cause cross-frame resource contention and transient kernel occupancy spikes. This PR introduces the 'holo-surge' sequencer (a compact workload generator that composes deterministic priming, stochastic microbursts, and long-period envelope modulation) and a crossframe occupancy exporter that records per-step GPU occupancy signatures and correlates them with logical request frames for downstream reporters.

What this change does: adds a new sequencer (cmd/holo-surge) and a reporting pipeline that serializes occupancy traces in a compact delta format compatible with the benchmark-lab downstream stats pipeline. The exporter uses platform-specific probes (NVIDIA SMI sampling hook or synthetic occupancy estimator when SMI is unavailable) and annotates traces with primer-id, sequence-hash, and KV cache-hit ratio. The sequencer supports deterministic seeds, phase locking across parallel nodes, and variable inter-sequence offsets for interference testing.

Checklist: [x] design doc (ENG-3124) reviewed; [x] unit tests for sequencer RNG and phase-lock logic; [x] integration test that runs holo-surge against mini-cluster and validates exporter schema; [x] perf benchmark comparing p50/p95/p99 and occupancy histograms before/after (summary below); [ ] follow-up: dashboard panels and alerting queries (separate PR).

Testing and validation: ran 5 controlled cohorts on the perf-lab cluster (4x A100 nodes) with identical model and prompt mixes to reproduce production-like priming distribution. Motivation: we observed sustained mid-tail p95 instability when replaying multi-sequence conversational traffic with interleaved priming and varied KV cache hits. Existing synthetic runners produce either Poisson-like bursts or deterministic ramps; they do not emulate the fractal-like, multi-scale surges we see in production that cause cross-frame resource contention and transient kernel occupancy spikes. This PR introduces the 'holo-surge' sequencer (a compact workload generator that composes deterministic priming, stochastic microbursts, and long-period envelope modulation) and a crossframe occupancy exporter that records per-step GPU occupancy signatures and correlates them with logical request frames for downstream reporters.

What this change does: adds a new sequencer (cmd/holo-surge) and a reporting pipeline that serializes occupancy traces in a compact delta format compatible with the benchmark-lab downstream stats pipeline. The exporter uses platform-specific probes (NVIDIA SMI sampling hook or synthetic occupancy estimator when SMI is unavailable) and annotates traces with primer-id, sequence-hash, and KV cache-hit ratio. The sequencer supports deterministic seeds, phase locking across parallel nodes, and variable inter-sequence offsets for interference testing.

title: Policy engine: compatibility guardrails for quantization profiles + safe fallback
preview: Motivation: Route-level quantization profile enablement must be safe-by-default. This PR adds compatibility evaluation inside the policy engine so that any resolved quantization profile ref is deterministically accepted/rejected based on (a) model family/variant constraints, (b) GPU SKU class support, and (c) max context / sequence-length regime constraints. When a profile is incompatible, policy resolution falls back to baseline precision (i.e., quantization disabled) and emits explicit metrics/loggable reason codes; default behavior is unchanged when quantization is not enabled on the route. Scope: This change is in the control-plane policy resolution path (routing policy engine), not runtime kernel selection. The runtime still receives a resolved decision (apply profile X vs baseline). Design notes: (1) Determinism: compatibility evaluation uses only stable inputs available at policy time (route config + resolved model_id/model_variant + deployment hardware class + requested max_tokens/context caps) and does not depend on live capacity state. (2) Explicit reason codes: on fallback, policy attaches a `quant_profile_resolution_outcome` and `quant_profile_incompat_reason` to the resolved route policy result; downstream components can tag logs/traces with the resolved outcome. (3) Safety: Incompatibility results in fallback to baseline precision rather than hard failure; the only hard failure is for malformed/unknown profile refs during config validation (used by Console preview). (4) Guardrails: GPU support is expressed as coarse SKU classes (e.g., `nvidia_a10`, `nvidia_l4`, `nvidia_a100`, `nvidia_h100`) to avoid per-SKU churn; max context constraints enforce `profile.max_context_tokens >= route.max_context_tokens` and optionally a stricter cap for mixed-precision kernels. (5) Observability: adds counters for fallback rate by reason and a gauge for enabled routes (as seen by policy evaluation). Implementation details: - Adds `QuantProfileCompatibilityEvaluator` with pure functions and table-driven rules. - Adds normalization layer mapping fleet GPU identifiers to `GpuSkuClass` used by policy. - Implements compatibility checks: model family match (e.g., `llama3` profile cannot be applied to `mistral`), variant allowlist (some profiles are `*-instruct` only), GPU SKU class membership, and max context token ceilings. - Adds safe fallback: if `route.quantization_profile_ref` resolves but is incompatible, policy returns baseline quantization mode and sets `quant_profile_applied=false` plus reason. - Adds config validation endpoint support hook: when called in "validate" mode, returns structured error messages (used by Console to show warnings/errors before save). - Adds metrics: `policy_quant_profile_fallback_total{reason=...}` and `policy_quant_profile_applied_total` plus debug logging at INFO with route_id + model_id + profile_id + reason. Tests: - Unit tests: table-driven cases across families, GPU classes, and context limits. - Property test: compatibility evaluation is stable across input ordering and is idempotent. - Integration-ish policy tests: route config with ref -> policy resolution -> expected outcome + metrics tags. Related work: - Route config field added in PR #18411. - Control plane endpoints in PR #18445 (consumes this evaluator during preview/validation). Follow-ups: - Consider expanding GPU class mapping for private/on-prem SKU reporting. - Once runtime emits `quant_profile_id` in traces, add dashboard panels broken down by `resolution_outcome` + `reason` (tracked in ENG-5817).

title: openai-bridge: forward client RNG-hints and support structured-schema mapping for new Responses variants
preview: Summary: Add support in the OpenAI compatibility bridge for forwarding client-specified randomness hints and an explicit rng_seed field, and introduce structured-schema mapping to better handle the newer Responses API variants. This change ensures client requests that include 'randomness_hint' or 'rng_seed' are preserved through the bridge and honored by the serving runtime when possible, and maps new structured output descriptors from OpenAI-style Responses to Redwood's structured-output parser.

title: Bootstrap audit exporter service skeleton + CI + local dev harness
preview:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)

title: Add config sync from control plane (polling + streaming) with tenant scoping + caching
preview:
Implements config sync from the control plane for the audit exporter service. This adds (1) tenant scoping so a given exporter instance only processes configs it is authorized to see, (2) a resilient polling loop with jitter/backoff and a long-poll “watch” mode when supported by the control plane, and (3) an in-memory snapshot cache with safe defaults so we do not accidentally start exporting for a tenant unless the config is explicitly enabled.

Motivation
- ENG-5139 created the exporter scaffold; we need an end-to-end way to pull export destinations/filters from the control plane without requiring restarts.
- ENG-5132 is landing CRUD + validation in the control plane; this PR wires up the consumer side.

Key behaviors
- Startup: exporter boots in “no tenants enabled” state until it successfully fetches a config snapshot.
- Polling: periodic snapshot fetch (default 10s with +/-20% jitter) with exponential backoff on failures.
- Streaming/watch: if the control plane advertises watch support, we keep an open long-poll to get revision notifications and then refresh the snapshot (keeps overhead low while staying responsive).
- Tenant scoping: requests use an exporter service identity; control plane response is already filtered to authorized tenants. Exporter additionally enforces that it will only instantiate pipelines for tenants present in the snapshot and will tear down pipelines if a tenant disappears from the snapshot.
- Safe defaults: configs without destinations, without enabled=true, or with invalid/missing credential refs are treated as disabled and surfaced in health/status metrics (no export attempts).
- Caching: configs are stored as an atomic snapshot; adapters read from a read-only view to avoid lock contention.

Notes
- This PR intentionally does not fetch any secret material. Destination configs refer to secret references (e.g., vault/KMS-managed identifiers) that adapters will resolve through the existing secret provider interface.
- Filtering semantics are not implemented here (ENG-5160). We pass through filter blobs to keep the boundary stable.

Follow-ups
- Wire config revision + export status endpoint (lag/last error) once the exporter metrics PR lands.
- Add more “thundering herd” mitigation once we have multi-replica deployments (per-instance staggered start already included).

title: Filtering engine v1: allowlist/denylist by category/action/resource
preview: Implements filtering engine v1 for SIEM-ready audit export (ENG-5160). This adds a small, explicit rule model (allowlist/denylist) that can be evaluated per-event in the exporter with deterministic semantics and safe defaults. Scope in this PR: (1) filter model + evaluation logic (categories/actions/resource predicates), (2) strict config validation + normalization (including “deny wins” and invalid rule rejection), and (3) comprehensive unit tests for tricky cases (empty filters, conflicting allow/deny, unknown categories, wildcard handling, resource/action normalization, timestamp boundary behavior). Motivation: we need customer-configurable filtering that is auditable, easy to reason about during incident response, and stable enough to map to UI + control-plane API without frequent behavior changes. Semantics (as implemented): The effective policy is evaluated in this order: validate -> normalize -> evaluate per event. If filters are absent/null, exporter passes all events (current beta default). If allowlist rules exist, an event must match at least one allow rule to be included. Deny rules always override allow rules (i.e., if an event matches any deny rule, it is excluded even if it also matches allow). Matching: category/action are exact match against canonical strings; resource matching supports resource.type exact match, and resource.id supports either exact match or a simple prefix pattern of the form "prefix:*" (to support common shapes like "workspace:*" without introducing a full glob/regex surface). If a rule specifies multiple fields, the rule matches only if all specified fields match (AND). If a filter list contains unknown categories/actions (not in the exporter’s current taxonomy allowlist), config is rejected and exporter will not start that tenant’s pipeline (surfaced as config error + status). This is intentional for beta to avoid silently dropping/including unexpected events; we can revisit “unknown = no match” for GA if we need forward-compat behavior. Notes: This PR is aligned with ADR-audit-export-filtering-model (allow/deny + deterministic evaluation) and keeps the API surface minimal. The control plane is expected to perform the same validation; exporter validation is defense-in-depth in case of stale/buggy configs. Follow-ups: (a) wire up exporter status endpoint to include “filter_validation_error” details (truncated/safe) for Console, (b) add microbench for high-volume match evaluation, (c) consider forward-compat for new categories (feature-flagged behavior) once schema v2 taxonomy is stable. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for matching + precedence - [x] Validation errors are structured and redact sensitive fields - [x] Metrics: counter for filtered_out_total by tenant + category (bounded cardinality) - [x] Docs snippet added to internal README for config examples - [ ] E2E coverage will land in ENG-5195 (separate PR)

title: Route config: add quantization_profile_ref (default-preserving)
preview: Summary: This PR extends the route configuration schema to include an optional quantization_profile_ref field. When the field is unset (or explicitly set to "default"), behavior is unchanged and the serving stack will continue to use the baseline (non-profile) execution path. Motivation: The Quantization Profile Library workstream needs a control-plane surface area to opt in per route (and later by region/tier) without changing tenant defaults. This PR is intentionally limited to route config wiring + compatibility-safe defaults; policy evaluation and runtime application are handled in follow-up PRs. Design notes: - New field: quantization_profile_ref (string). Allowed values in this PR are: unset/null, "default", or a concrete profile ref string (format validation in control plane comes later). - Default behavior: treat unset and "default" identically; downstream resolution returns "disabled" state for profile application. - Backwards compatibility: Existing routes deserialize cleanly; API responses omit the field unless explicitly set (to avoid unexpected diffs for customers mirroring config). - Serialization: Both REST JSON and internal protobuf/struct tags updated. - Audit/change tracking: This PR adds explicit diff output for the new field in route config change logs, but does not introduce new audit event types yet. Migration: Adds a schema migration to persist the new field in route_config storage with a NULL default. Includes a safe backfill job that is a no-op (leaves NULL) and can be rolled forward/back without rewriting large tables. API docs: Added inline docs in the route config API structs (Go) describing semantics, default handling, and examples. Testing: - Unit tests for (1) decode/encode stability, (2) patch semantics (field clearing vs unset), and (3) default equivalence between unset and "default". - Golden tests updated for route config snapshots. Rollout plan: Ship behind existing server-side feature gate (RouteConfigQuantizationRefWrite) for write paths only; read paths are safe because the field is optional and omitted by default. Once policy engine guardrails are merged, we can enable writes for internal accounts + design partners.

title: Emit metrics + tracing for Private backup/restore operations (with error categorization)
preview: Motivation: SRE/on-call and customers need a consistent way to answer “are backups running?” and “why did the last restore drill fail?” across VPC + on-prem Private installs. We already log backup/restore steps in the installer, but logs alone are hard to alert on and don’t support reliable aggregation. This PR adds: (1) structured Prometheus metrics for backup + restore operations (success/failure, duration, bytes, last success timestamps), (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans with consistent attributes for per-step visibility, and (3) explicit error categorization to drive dashboards/alerts and reduce pager noise. Scope: This instruments the control-plane backup/restore path (installer-driven + control-plane endpoints) and adds common helpers used by both “backup create” and “restore apply”. It does not change backup artifact format or encryption behavior. Metrics (Prometheus): - redwood_private_backup_runs_total{result,storage_backend,component,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{result,storage_backend,component} (histogram) - redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{result,storage_backend,component} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{storage_backend,component,format_version} (gauge) - redwood_private_restore_runs_total{result,source_backend,target_env,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_restore_duration_seconds_bucket{result,step} (histogram) - redwood_private_restore_validation_failures_total{check_name,error_category} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_restore_errors_total{operation,step,error_category} (counter; emitted on failure paths only) Tracing (OTel): Adds a top-level span per operation: “private.backup.create” and “private.restore.apply”, with child spans for steps: preflight, quiesce, snapshot, encrypt, upload, verify, bootstrap, migrate, validate. Spans include attributes: deployment_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, storage_backend, artifact_manifest_version, backup_id/restore_id, step, result, error_category. Error categorization: Introduces a shared enum + mapping helper that classifies common failure types in a stable way for alerting/reporting. Categories include: preflight_failed, quiesce_failed, snapshot_failed, encrypt_failed, upload_failed, download_failed, verify_failed, bootstrap_failed, migrate_failed, validation_failed, compatibility_failed, permission_denied, kms_unavailable, timeout, rate_limited, unknown. Mapping is conservative: if we can’t confidently classify, we emit unknown and preserve the raw error message in logs/traces (not as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. RBAC / permissions: No new privileges are required for the control plane, but installer pods/jobs must be allowed to emit metrics to the existing collector in Private installs. This PR reuses existing OTEL exporter wiring and the in-cluster Prometheus scrape config. Testing: - Unit tests for error classification mapping and label sanitization - Integration test in staging that runs a forced failure (bad bucket creds) and asserts metrics increments + spans have error_category - Manual validation: ran backup create / restore apply in dev VPC env; verified dashboard panels populate (see follow-up in observability-pack PR). Notes on cardinality: - No metric labels include backup_id, restore_id, customer name, or raw error strings - deployment_id is included only as a tracing attribute (not a metric label) - component label is constrained to a small set (control_plane_db, control_plane_config, k8s_manifests, audit_logs_export) - step label is constrained to known step names and used only on histogram for restore duration per step (bounded) Follow-ups: - Alert rules + dashboards land in observability-pack (PR-94) - SLO definitions tracked in slo-toolkit (PR-67) - Backup status endpoint is in PR-24318 (separate) which will consume these metrics for UI surfacing

title: Private: expose backup status endpoints from control plane
preview:
Motivation: Private customers (and our own SRE/Solutions workflows) need a consistent way to surface “backup readiness” without scraping CronJob logs or inferring state from storage backends. This PR adds control-plane API endpoints that report backup status and last-success timestamps per tenant/installation, with strict authz and safe redaction (no bucket names, object keys, or secret material). This unblocks Console UI + automated restore drills to gate on “recent successful backup” and to page on backup job regressions.

What’s included: Adds a BackupStatus read model persisted by the backup orchestrator after each run (success/failure), keyed by tenant_id and environment_id; Read-only endpoints under the Private API surface; Includes timestamps (last_success_at, last_attempt_at), status enum, last_error_category + last_error_message (truncated), artifact metadata (format_version, bytes_total), and a computed “staleness” field based on configured schedule; RBAC scopes: tenant:read plus a new private:backup_status:read for installer/service automation; Optional cluster-wide endpoint for platform operators (requires internal operator role); Adds OpenAPI spec + SDK client stub used by Console; Adds audit log events for reads of backup status (low volume) and writes on backup completion.

Endpoints: GET /v1/private/tenants/{tenant_id}/backup/status -> returns latest status record for that tenant; GET /v1/private/backup/status -> operator-only list endpoint with pagination/filtering by status/staleness (intended for internal fleet dashboards; not exposed in customer docs yet).

Non-goals / out of scope: No “trigger backup” endpoint (installer/cronjob remains source of truth); No exposure of artifact locations (bucket/key) or encryption/key IDs; No attempt to infer state by reading object storage; This does not change backup/restore format.

Security + privacy notes: last_error_message is truncated to 512 chars and scrubbed via existing log redaction utility; artifact_metadata excludes backend identifiers; list endpoint is operator-only and excluded from external docs generation.

Testing: Unit tests for status model serialization, authz gates, and redaction; API integration tests covering success and failure flows; Added a contract test asserting the response schema remains backwards compatible (fields only added, never removed) to support Console; Load test sanity: list endpoint paginates and enforces max page size.

Rollout plan: Ship behind feature flag private.backupStatusEndpoints (default on for staging, off for prod) with a follow-up to enable for Private GA once Console is ready; SRE to validate via restore drill automation and dashboard wiring.

Checklist: [x] OpenAPI updated; [x] Authz reviewed (Marcus); [x] Audit logging for reads/writes; [x] Metrics emitted on write path (status_write_success, status_write_latency_ms); [x] Docs note added to internal runbook; [x] Backwards compatible response contract.

Refs: Linear ENG-9826/ENG-9827; related installer work in redwood-private-installer PR-482/PR-489; observability pack PR-94 depends on status fields last_success_at + staleness_minutes.

title: Add trafficctl emergency GSLB weight setter for api.redwoodinference.com
preview:
### Context
During game day and recent regional brownouts, the fastest safe mitigation is to shift Hosted API traffic between regions without waiting for Terraform applies. We currently do this via manual console operations, which is error-prone.

### What this PR does
- Introduces `trafficctl`, an audited CLI wrapper around our GSLB provider API
- Adds commands to:
  - read current targets + weights
  - set weights for api.redwoodinference.com
  - set record TTL (temporary)
- Enforces RBAC via the `infra-traffic-admin` role
- Writes an audit log event for every invocation (who, what record, old/new weights)

### Usage
```
trafficctl gslb get --record api.redwoodinference.com
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 60
trafficctl gslb set-weights --record api.redwoodinference.com --weights us-east-1-gw=0,us-west-2-gw=100
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 300
```

### Rollback
- Disable the `trafficctl` GitHub Actions release workflow and revert to manual console operations
- No data-plane changes are required to roll back this PR

### Changed areas
- `cmd/trafficctl/*`
- `pkg/gslb/*`
- `pkg/auditlog/*`
- `rbac/policies/infra-traffic-admin.yaml`
- `docs/runbooks/global-traffic-routing-controls.md` (synced to Confluence by docs bot)

### Review notes
- Hannah: please verify audit event fields match incident review needs
- Alex: verify gateway target naming conventions (us-east-1-gw, us-west-2-gw)

### Review conversation (excerpt)
Hannah Schmitt: Can we ensure we log *previous weights* as well as the new weights? That's critical for rollback during an incident.

Dev Patel: Yep, updated `auditlog.Event` to include `old_weights` and `new_weights` as strings (serialized).

Ethan Park: Any safety rails to prevent accidental 100% to an unknown target?

Dev Patel: Added validation: target must be in the allowlist for the record; otherwise command aborts.

Alex Martinez: Looks good. Please add a short note that TTL should be restored after the cutover.

Dev Patel: Added to docs + examples.

### Merge
- Squash merged into main
- CI: build/test/lint passed

title: Add sparse error annotator and CI autorepair hook for flaky prompt-sets
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a lightweight 'sparse error annotator' and an accompanying CI autorepair hook aimed at reducing noisy, low-density failures in prompt-set regression checks. The annotator detects when a regression signal is concentrated in very few prompts or is likely caused by brittle input formatting; the autorepair hook runs a set of deterministic canonicalizers on flagged examples and re-executes the failing tests in CI. If the example passes post-repair the check is quarantined as a probable flake instead of failing the entire job. Motivation: We saw recurring alerts in canary / smoke evals where 1-2 prompts per prompt-set caused CI failures that were later determined to be formatting or tokenization edge-cases. These false positives interrupt rollouts and cause unnecessary investigations. This change reduces noisiness while preserving sensitivity to real regressions.

title: Fork-and-Join rollout controller with stable session affinity
preview:
Summary: Introduce a new Fork-and-Join rollout controller and CRD (ForkJoinRollout) that allows operators to split traffic into multiple parallel rollout paths (forks), run independent validation windows, then atomically join a winning path back into the primary serving graph while preserving stable session affinity and KV cache locality. Motivation: Some experiments require running two or more independent model variants for overlapping cohorts and then merging the best-performing variant into production without user-visible churn or cache thrash. Existing canary and shadow primitives support linear progressive rollouts but do not provide first-class semantics for forked experiments and deterministic joins. This PR implements the control plane and runtime hooks to enable fork-and-join workflows with safety fences and rollback tracebacks. Changes: Commits: 1) Add CRD and API surface for ForkJoinRollout with fields: forks[], joinPolicy{windowMs, quorum, metricsGates}, affinity{cookieHeader, hashSalt}, rollbackTraceTTL 2) Implement controller reconciliation loop: deterministic cohort hashing, fork assignment, per-fork observation windows, metric aggregation, join decision, and transactional switch-over. 3) Runtime integration: routing plugin that honors session affinity cookie/header to route existing sessions consistently to their assigned fork across restarts. 4) KV cache migration helpers that pin or warm keys for transitioning cohorts to avoid cache cold-start spikes at join. 5) Metrics and alerting: new rollout.* metrics, join_decision_latency, affinity_mismatch_count, cache_warmup_effect. 6) E2E tests: simulated traffic harness, chaos test (controller restarts), and load test to validate no-traffic-glitch join. Files changed (high-level):
- k8s/crds/forkjoinrollout.yaml: new CRD and schema.
- controllers/forkjoin/controller.go: main reconciliation implementation and state machine.
- controllers/forkjoin/affinity.go: deterministic hashing and cookie utility.
- runtime/router/plugin_forkjoin.go: router plugin that preserves affinity and implements transactional join.
- runtime/cache/warmup.go: helpers to migrate/warm keys during join.
- observability/metrics_rollouts.prom: new metrics.
- tests/e2e/forkjoin/harness.go: traffic harness and validators.
- docs/deployments/forkjoin.md: operator guide and examples.
- scripts/bench/forkjoin_load_test.sh: benchmark harness configs.
- go.mod/go.sum and small refactors in existing rollout packages.
Review thread summary:
- Ravi: "Controller looks solid but we need stronger invariants around rollbackTraceTTL to avoid orphaned sessions." Author (Maya): "Added TTL enforcement in reconciliation and a periodic sweep task; documented behaviour."
- Sofia: "Affinity cookie name collisions with other RBAC cookies are possible in some proxies." Author: "Namespace-prefixed cookie names and a proxy integration guideline added to docs."
- Ethan: "Will join be atomic from a client perspective? What about in-flight KV cache updates?" Author: "Join is transactional at routing layer: existing sessions continue hitting assigned variant until their TTL expires; new sessions post-join get routed to joined variant. KV warmup reduces cold-start; we added a brief overlap window where both variants are served to smooth cache state."
Requested changes and iterations:
- Added unit tests for hashing function and cookie header fallbacks.
- Added additional metrics for debugging affinity mismatches.
- Tightened concurrency handling in controller reconciliation to avoid double-assigning cohorts under controller duplication.
CI: Initial CI run flagged a flaky e2e test on the harness timeout.
Fixes: increased harness timeout and added retries for the chaos step.
Final CI status: all checks passed on rerun (unit, integration, e2e, lint, go vet).
Performance validation: Microbenchmarks show median routing latency increase of <0.6ms when affinity cookie is present. Load tests demonstrate join window traffic spike mitigated by KV warmups in 95th percentile CPU usage.
Migration notes:
- The new CRD is opt-in; cluster admins must apply k8s/crds/forkjoinrollout.yaml.
- Default behavior is disabled unless a ForkJoinRollout object is created.
- Operators should configure affinity.hashSalt to ensure deterministic cohort assignment across controller upgrades.
Rollback behavior:
- The controller records a rollback trace when a fork fails metrics gates; traces are retained for rollbackTraceTTL and can be used to replay traffic for debugging.
- Automatic rollback will reroute new sessions away from the failing fork and optionally quarantine traffic via shadowing for post-mortem.
Backwards compatibility and safety:
- No changes to existing Canary or TrafficSplit CRDs.
- Router plugin is feature-flagged; when disabled, the routing falls back to prior routing semantics.
- CRD schema includes strict validation to prevent malformed rollouts.
Example usage: see docs/deployments/forkjoin.md for YAML samples demonstrating a 2-fork A/B-like experiment where traffic is split by hashed user_id, independent metrics gates are applied, and the controller performs a join after both forks exceed thresholds.
Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals from reviewers and green CI.
Related work: complements the A/B and canary toolset and provides a safer path for multi-variant experiments that require atomic join semantics.
Linear: ENG-4210 (Design and API), ENG-4278 (Runtime router & cache warmup).
Notes for on-call: If affinity_mismatch_count spikes post-deploy, roll forward with controller disable flag to revert to legacy routing, or apply a focused CR to adjust affinity.hashSalt. This PR is intentionally conservative in defaults and feature-flagged to allow incremental rollout.

title: asymmetric-concurrency-skew-generator-and-delta-reporter
preview:
Motivation: production traffic often exhibits heavy user skew where a small fraction of sessions produce high token bursts and long-lived KV cache pressure. Existing synthetic runners in benchmark-lab model uniform sequence mixes or simple hot-spot priors; we need a compact harness that can: 1) generate temporally correlated bursty sessions with configurable skew parameters; 2) drive the serving runtime under asymmetric concurrency to exercise scheduling and KV cache eviction behaviors; and 3) produce a compact delta report that correlates latency/regression deltas with kernel-level sample points and concurrency slices so that regressions can be triaged to scheduling vs kernel choices.

This PR implements: a) an "asymmetric-concurrency-skew" workload generator (bench/chaos/asymmetric_skew.go + python harness) supporting: percentiles-of-sessions that become "bursters", configurable burst lengths, inter-burst backoff distributions, and an option to pin bursts to a subset of model shards to provoke scheduling hotspots; b) an interaction-replay adapter that converts production session traces into parametric generators for deterministic replay; c) a delta-reporter pipeline (reporting/delta_reporter.py) that ingests per-request telemetry and aggregates per-concurrency-slice p50/p95/p99, token-rate, and a new metric "tail_concurrency_skew"; d) a small profiling baseline target and flamepoint mapper that links sampled kernel stacks to delta report cohorts for quick root-cause inspection.

Checklist:
- [x] Workload generator implementation + unit tests
- [x] Replay adapter with deterministic seeding
- [x] Delta reporter with CSV and JSON outputs (schema documented)
- [x] CI integration: unit tests + small e2e smoke job in bench-lab CI matrix
- [x] Minimal docs: README with knobs and example Linear ticket templates

Changed areas (high-level): ["bench/chaos/asymmetric_skew.go", "bench/harness/replay_adapter.py", "reporting/delta_reporter.py", "reporting/schema/v1.json", "bench/tests/test_asymmetric_skew.py", "bench/ci/bench_e2e_smoke.yml", "docs/bench/asymmetric_skew.md"]

- Priya Shah: "Added deterministic seeding and updated README with guidance for KV checkpointing and how to interpret tail_concurrency_skew. See latest commit."
- Marta Kline: "The delta report ties into flamepoints nicely, but we should limit the default retention of sampled stacks for bench runs to avoid storage blowup."
- Priya Shah: "Implemented a sampling cap and summarized the retention tradeoffs in the docs; default = 1000 stacks per run cohort."
- Diego Alvarez: "Approving once CI green and tests cover model-pin multicast path."

CI summary: all unit tests and bench smoke matrix passed on first run except one flaky test in bench/tests/test_kvcache_occupancy.py which was re-run and passed; full matrix green. Checks: ["golangci-lint: pass", "pytest: pass", "bench-smoke-e2e: pass", "docs-lint: pass"]

Performance notes and findings included in the PR: the asymmetric workload reproduces a scheduling-induced p99 spike when bursters exceed 8% of sessions for our current scheduler config; with the new scheduling hint (experimental) the p99 reduced by ~22% in a 4-GPU shard layout. The delta reporter makes it straightforward to attribute the regression to thread-starvation during KV cache eviction windows vs kernel selection for attention.

Merge notes: squash-merged after addressing reviewers' comments and adding seeding; release notes generated and attached.

Related work: ENG-4021 (scheduling experiments), bench-lab issue #413 (need for replayable skewed workloads), SUP-1291 (support ticket from customer experiencing tail spikes).

title: credential-fuse-pausable-stream-lifecycle-tri-sdk-bump
preview: Summary: This PR implements a cross-language ergonomic surface for pausable/resumable streaming consumers, and a lightweight credential handoff mechanism we call 'credential-fuse' that reduces reauth churn during short-lived auth windows. Motivation: several teams reported latency spikes and redundant reauth calls during bursty streaming workloads; debugging showed frequent credential refresh races and lack of a clean pause/resume lifecycle on client streams. Goals: 1) provide pausable stream primitives that preserve local cursor/offset state, allow explicit credential handoff with minimal RPC, and provide telemetry for retry decisioning; 2) harmonize ergonomic APIs across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs so examples and migration paths are consistent; 3) surface retry hints and a small set of typed retry presets to make opt-in behavior explicit. What changed (high level): - SDK Python: added StreamController.pause()/resume(), StreamContext.attach_credential_fuse(), small refactor of session manager to support short-lived fuse leases, new RetryPreset enum and telemetry labels (retry.reason, retry.hint). - SDK TypeScript: introduced StreamHandle.pause()/resume(), exported types for CredentialFuse and RetryPresets; simplified backoff constructor to accept named presets. - SDK Go: added stream.PausableStreamer interface, CredFuse struct and AttachFuse helper; exported RetryPreset constants and telemetry tags. - Core API compatibility: extended transport handshake to optionally carry a short-lived fuse token and a resume cursor; compatible fallback maintains old handshake shape. - Telemetry/CI: added new retry decision metrics (sdk.retry.decisions), fuse churn counters (sdk.auth.fuse_attempts), and stream lifecycle spans. - Docs/examples: updated tri-sdk examples repository with pause/resume demo and recommended retry presets for conversational and low-latency workloads. - Tests: added unit and integration tests for pause/resume semantics, credential transfer safety, and telemetry emission. Backwards compatibility: default behavior unchanged; fuse usage is opt-in. If server does not support fuse header, SDKs fall back to standard refresh flow. Migration notes: see README updates in SDK folders — primary change for integrators is replacing manual reconnect logic with StreamController.pause/resume when a transient auth flow is desired. Security notes: fuse tokens are single-use bearer tokens with TTL <= 30s and are logged only as hashes in telemetry. Checklist: - [x] API design review with platform (ENG-2419) - [x] Unit tests for Python/TS/Go - [x] Integration e2e against staging runtime - [x] Telemetry dashboards updated - [x] Docs + examples updated - [x] Version bumps and changelogs updated Performance: soldering fuse reduced reauth RPCs by ~67% in our internal canary (conversational agent workload) and improved median p95 reconnect latency by ~18ms in burst tests. Rollout plan: feature gated by server capability flag. We'll ship SDKs immediately; server-side acceptor will be rolled to staging for 48h canary, then gradual rollout to prod. Related work: builds on earlier retry-presets and session-lease efforts; references: Linear ENG-2419, PM-735.

title: Consensus-led A/B Failover with Explainable Decision Traces
preview:
Motivation: Canary/A-B promotions in multi-tenant services often react to single-signal thresholds (latency or error rate). That produces noisy rollbacks and opaque decisions for downstream SREs and product owners. This change introduces a lightweight consensus-driven failover controller that aggregates orthogonal signals (p50/p95 latency deltas, availability/error spikes, semantic drift via embedding distance, and synthetic smoke checks) across cohorts to make an explainable pass/fail decision for A/B promotions and canaries.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a new controller (consensus_ab_controller) that: collects metric shards from multiple telemetry sources, computes a configurable quorum using weighted voters, produces a deterministic decision (+trace) for promote/rollback/freeze, and exposes the decision trace to the rollout logs and the Console. Includes a rehearsal harness for shadow traffic that simulates failover decisions offline and an autotune adapter that suggests safe max traffic fractions based on historical variance. Also extends existing rollout API to accept a "decision_trace_url" and a short JSON-LD decision schema for auditability.

Why now: We observed two incidents where a single noisy p95 spike caused a broad rollback even though other signals were healthy. The consensus model reduces sensitivity while improving debuggability for postmortems.

Checklist: - [x] Controller implementation and unit tests - [x] Integration tests with simulated shadow traffic harness - [x] Instrumentation: decision traces + Console hookup - [x] Migration guide and API docs - [x] Performance bench (end-to-end latency impact < 4ms median)

Compatibility/Migration notes: The new controller is opt-in via rollout.policy.type = "consensus". Existing rollout behaviors remain unchanged unless a customer switches policy. Decision traces are stored as ephemeral queryable objects for 7 days by default (configurable per tenant).

Related: Linear ENG-4872 for design doc, SUP-1143 for customer incident follow-up.
